questions.question: Explain the concept of a hash table and its advantages over other data structures.
keyPoints: Hash table is a data structure mapping keys to values using hash functions
Provides O(1) average-case time for insert, delete, lookup
Uses direct addressing via computed index
More efficient than linear search O(n) or binary search O(log n) for associative lookup

questions.question: What is a collision? Describe two techniques for resolving collisions in hash tables.
keyPoints: Collision occurs when two different keys produce the same hash value
Two main categories: Open Addressing and Separate Chaining
Separate chaining: uses linked lists at each bucket
Open addressing: stores elements in table using probing methods

questions.question: Define load factor and explain its significance in hash tables.
keyPoints: Load factor (α) = n/m where n = elements, m = buckets
Measures how full the hash table is
Lower load factor = fewer collisions = better performance
Typical threshold for rehashing is α ≈ 0.7

questions.question: Explain linear probing and discuss its drawbacks.
keyPoints: Linear probing: if collision at h(k), check (h(k)+i) mod m
Simple to implement
Problem: Primary clustering - long runs of filled slots
Performance degrades as table fills up

questions.question: What is rehashing? When and why is it performed?
keyPoints: Rehashing: creating a new, larger hash table and re-inserting all elements
Performed when load factor exceeds threshold (typically 0.7)
Maintains O(1) average operations by reducing collisions
Table size usually doubled

questions.question: Compare separate chaining and open addressing collision resolution techniques.
keyPoints: Separate chaining: linked list per bucket, handles overflow
Open addressing: all elements in table, uses probing
Separate chaining uses extra memory for pointers
Open addressing has faster access but clustering issues

questions.question: Describe the properties of a good hash function.
keyPoints: Deterministic: same key always produces same hash
Uniform distribution: minimizes clustering
Fast to compute
Should handle various key types effectively

questions.question: Explain double hashing as a collision resolution technique. How does it help in avoiding clustering problems? Derive the probe function and analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Double hashing uses two hash functions: h1(k) and h2(k)
Probe function: h(k,i) = (h1(k) + i × h2(k)) mod m
h2(k) should be non-zero and relatively prime to m
Avoids primary clustering by using variable step size
Secondary hash function spreads keys more uniformly
Time complexity approaches O(1/(1-α)) with good hashing

questions.question: Write an algorithm for inserting an element into a hash table using quadratic probing. Analyze its time complexity and discuss the conditions required to guarantee finding an empty slot.
keyPoints: Algorithm: start at h(k), probe at (h(k)+i²) mod m for i=0,1,2...
Time complexity: O(1/(1-α)) average case
To guarantee finding empty slot: table size should be prime and α < 0.5
Or use table size as power of 2 with certain conditions
Quadratic probing reduces primary clustering

questions.question: Explain universal hashing. How does it provide worst-case average performance? Discuss how it differs from deterministic hashing.
keyPoints: Universal hashing: randomly select hash function from a family
Family H contains functions where collision probability is ≤ 1/m
Provides probabilistic guarantee against worst-case inputs
Unlike deterministic: same input may hash differently in different runs
Expected time for any set of n keys is O(n) for O(m) table size
Useful when input distribution is unknown or adversarial

questions.question: Design a hash table implementation with separate chaining for collision resolution. Include algorithms for insertion, deletion, and searching. Analyze the time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Use array of linked list heads
Insert: compute hash, add to front of list (O(1) avg)
Search: compute hash, traverse list (O(n/m) = O(α) avg)
Delete: search then remove node from list
Space: O(n + m) for n elements and m buckets
Average case: O(1 + α), worst case: O(n) if all keys collide
